design_philosophy.core_loop: Build cities, research technologies, manage resources (production, gold, science), expand territory, compete diplomatically and militarily against AI civilizations across millennia
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing expansion vs. consolidation; competing for finite resources and technological advantages against AI opponents with distinct personalities
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Leading a civilization from ancient times through the space age; making grand strategic decisions about governance, culture, and military might across centuries
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Establishes the 4X genre template (eXplore, eXpand, eXploit, eXterminate); emphasizes agency in city placement, research priorities, and government type; historical flavor through leader personalities without mechanical differentiation; micromanagement-lite approach requiring deliberate spatial and economic planning

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects civilization, difficulty setting affects game length (easiest: ends at AD 2100 with future techs); implied adjustable challenge through AI opponent selection and game settings

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree with sequential unlocks (pottery, wheel, alphabet → nuclear fission, spaceflight); city improvements and unit types unlock through research
progression.unlock_mechanism: Chronological technology advancement; first civilization to discover a tech gains temporary advantage; terrain improvements (mines, irrigation, roads, railroads) scale with era
replayability_hooks: Multiple historical civilizations with distinct leader personalities (Gandhi, Napoleon, Montezuma, etc.)
Random world generation or Play on Earth variants with different starting resources
Varying AI opponent combinations and difficulty settings
Multiple victory conditions (conquest, cultural, diplomatic, space race implied)

world_building_techniques: Historical civilization selection provides thematic context (leader names, city names, music heralds)
Technology tree mimics real historical advancement (Bronze Age → Space Age framework)
Barbarian encounters establish world hostility; exploring reveals habitable and resource-rich lands
Diplomatic relationships between civilizations with named historical leaders create implicit narrative tension

faction_indicators: Selectable historical civilizations (Americans, Aztecs, Babylonians, Chinese, Egyptians, English, French, Germans, Greeks, Indians, Mongols, Romans, Russians, Zulus)
Named AI leaders embodying civilization traits (Aztecs: fiercely expansionist and wealthy; others display characteristic behaviors)
Government type selection reflects player ideology (implied: monarchy, republic, democracy, etc.)
Diplomatic alliances and warfare create shifting political blocs
danger_signals: Barbarian unit spawning from unclaimed territory signals frontier vulnerability
AI civilization expansion and military unit production indicate hostility
Technological race creates power imbalance when opponent gains key early advantage
Taxation and resource depletion create internal pressure
environmental_storytelling: Terrain transformation (mines, irrigation, roads connecting cities) visualizes economic development
City placement on rivers, mountains, and coastlines reflects strategic resource management
Empty map gradually fills with competing civilizations—visual representation of imperial competition
Terrain huts harbor barbarians and resources; exploration reduces barbarian threat over time

sources_used: wikipedia: Civilization (video game) - Gameplay and Development sections
